quotes.speaker: Jane Monheit
quotes.text: Yeah. Not while I was pregnant. I sang great when I was pregnant. Even when I was nine months pregnant. I did a gig at Carnegie Hall, at the recital hall at Carnegie, a duo gig with my pianist, maybe a week or ten days before my son was born, just out to here like waddling in flats and just singing, no problem. But the problem is you have this baby in you and it’s pressing on your diaphragm and then you have the baby and all the muscles are just kind of like flabby. And you know it takes a while to get your breathing back together and hormonal changes can affect your voices. And I have just recently been going through a period of vocal difficulty, you know my voice gets tired very, very easily. It’s been like seeing doctors and coaches and the whole thing, and every singer goes through this at some point and it’s very, very common to go through this sort of thing after having a child, especially now that my voice is being used more than ever. Not only are we on the road constantly, and jazz singers sing probably more than anyone else, because we’ll do like twelve shows a week. Everyone knows that I want to be on Broadway one day and people always say to me, “Oh, Jane, eight shows a week that’s a lot, can you handle it?” And I’m like, “Honey, I do twelve in six days all the time. And I fly with a 2-year-old with me.” You know, I’m like, “Please, I can handle it.” So we really sing a lot. And something that my brilliant voice teacher Joan Lader said to me recently that I never thought about it, she said for a singer like me a show is very stressful because I change genre constantly, and so it’s like several different kinds of singing. It’s not just like I’m doing one thing and staying in one place in terms of technique throughout the show, it’s constantly changing and she said that can be very stressful. And I never thought about it. So you know, I’m talking to my son all day, I’m singing to him all day, I’m doing two shows at night sometimes, there’s sound check or rehearsing, there’s always something going on. There’s traveling. There’s sleep deprivation. So it’s a lot, it’s a lot. Plus I speak improperly. I’m trying to learn to speak properly and I just can’t.
quotes.caption: Jane Monheit's duo recital with Michael Kanan
quotes.doc_title: Jane Monheit interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, August 20, 2010
